volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 1.1.75 - Section 1.2.145)

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: Simulato me amare .
sections.translation: Do pretend that you are in love with me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75b
sections.original: Vtrum égo istuc iócon adsimulem an serio ?
sections.translation: Whether should I be pretending that in jest , or seriously ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.76
sections.original: Heia , hoc agere meliust . miles quom huc adveniat , te volo
sections.translation: Well said ! better to do the last . When the Captain comes here , I want you [*] to embrace me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.77b
sections.original: Quid eo mi opus est ?
sections.translation: What need is there of my doing that ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.77c
sections.original: Vt ille te videat volo .
sections.translation: I want him to see you . I know what I ’ m about ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.78b
sections.original: Et pol égo scio quid metuo . sed quid ais ?
sections.translation: And I , i ’ faith , I know not what I ’ m in fear of . But what say you —?

sections.label: Section 1.1.78c
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter now ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.79
sections.original: Quid si apud te eveniát desubito prandium aut potatio
sections.translation: Well , suppose perchance on a sudden a breakfast or a drinking bout ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: forte aut cena , ut solet in istis fieri conciliabulis ,
sections.translation: or else a dinner , should take place at your house , just as is the wont in such places of resort ; where , then , should I take my place ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.81b
sections.original: Ápud me , mi anime , ut lepidus cum lepida accubet .
sections.translation: Near myself , my life , that with a she wit a he wit may be reclining at the repast . A place here , at our house , should you come late , is always at your service . When you wish right merrily to disport yourself , my rosebud , you say to me , Do let me enjoy myself to-day , I ’ ll provide you a delightful place where it may be so .

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: Rapidus fluvius est hic , non hac temere transiri potest .
sections.translation: Here is a rapid stream ; not without hazard can this way be passed .

sections.label: Section 1.1.86
sections.original: Atque ecastor apud hunc fluvium áliquid perdundumst tibi .
sections.translation: And , by my troth , something must you lose amid this stream . ( Aloud .) Give me your hand and follow me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.87b
sections.original: Aha , minime .
sections.translation: O , by no means .

sections.label: Section 1.1.87c
sections.original: Quid ita ?
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.87d
sections.original: Quia istoc in lecebrosius
sections.translation: Because to a man in his youth nothing can be more alluring than these — night , women , wine .

sections.label: Section 1.1.89
sections.original: Age igitur , equidem pol nihili facio nisi causa tua .
sections.translation: Away , then , with you ; for my part , I don ’ t at all care for it , but for your own sake .

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: ille quidem hanc abducet ; tú nullus ádfueris , si non lubet .
sections.translation: The Captain , then , shall take her off ; don ’ t you be present at all if you don ’ t choose .

sections.label: Section 1.1.91
sections.original: Sumne autem nihili , qui nequeam ingenio moderari meo ?
sections.translation: And am I a thing of nothing , who cannot moderate my own passions ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.92
sections.original: Quid est quod metuas ?
sections.translation: What ’ s there for you to fear ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.92b
sections.original: Nihil est , nugae . mulier , tibi me emancupo :
sections.translation: There ’ s nothing ; all nonsense . Madam , I resign myself to you . I ’ m yours ; to you do I devote my services .

sections.label: Section 1.1.93b
sections.original: . nunc ego te facere hoc volo .
sections.translation: You are a dear man . Now I wish you to do this . To-day I want to give a welcome entertainment [*] to my sister .

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: eo tibi argentum iubebo iam intus ecferri foras ;
sections.translation: For that purpose I shall at once order the money [*] to be brought you from in-doors . Do you take care and cater for us a splendid entertainment .

sections.label: Section 1.1.97
sections.original: Ego opsonabo , nam id flagitium meum sit , mea te gratia
sections.translation: No , I ’ ll stand treat ; for it would be a shame , that on my account you both should take trouble for me , and by reason of that trouble should pay the expense from your own means .

sections.label: Section 1.1.99
sections.original: At ego nolo dare te quicquam .
sections.translation: But I can ’ t allow you to give anything .

sections.label: Section 1.1.99b
sections.original: Sine .
sections.translation: Do let me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.99c
sections.original: Sino equidem , si lubet .
sections.translation: Well , I ’ ll let you , if you choose .

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: propera , amabo .
sections.translation: Prithee , do make haste .

sections.label: Section 1.1.100b
sections.original: Prius hic adero quam te amare desinam .—
sections.translation: I shall be here again , before I cease to love you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.101
sections.original: Bene me accipies advenientem , mea soror .
sections.translation: You entertain me pleasantly upon my return , my sister .

sections.label: Section 1.1.101b
sections.original: Quid ita , obsecro ?
sections.translation: How so , prithee ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.102
sections.original: Quia piscatus meo quidem animo hic tibi hodie evenit bonus .
sections.translation: Because , in my way of thinking , this day a lucky haul has fallen to your lot .

sections.label: Section 1.1.103
sections.original: Meus ille quidemst . tibi nunc operam dabo de Mnesilocho , soror ,
sections.translation: He ’ s mine , assuredly . Now , sister , with respect to Mnesilochus , I ’ ll give you my aid [*] , that here at home you may be receiving gold , rather than be going hence together with the Captain .

sections.label: Section 1.1.105
sections.original: Cupio .
sections.translation: That ’ s my desire .

sections.label: Section 1.1.105b
sections.original: Dabitur opera . aqua calet : eamus hinc intro , ut laves .
sections.translation: My aid shall be given you . The water ’ s warm ; let ’ s go in , that you may bathe . For , as you have travelled on board ship , you are faint , I think .

sections.label: Section 1.1.106b
sections.original: Aliquantum , soror .
sections.translation: A little so , sister . ( PISTOCLERUS is seen at a distance .) Besides , he ’ s beginning to cause I don ’ t know what bustle . Let ’ s begone hence .

sections.label: Section 1.1.108
sections.original: Sequere hac igitur me intro in lectum , ut sedes lassitudinem .
sections.translation: Follow me this way in-doors to bathe , that you may relieve your weariness .

sections.label: Section 1.2.109
sections.original: Iam dudum , Pistoclere , tacitus te sequor ,
sections.translation: For some time , Pistoclerus , I ’ ve been following you in silence [*] ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: expectans quas tu res hoc ornatu geras .
sections.translation: watching what you were doing in this dress [*] . For , so may the Gods favour me , even Lycurgus himself [*] seems to me as if he could be led into debauchery here . Whither now are you betaking yourself hence in an opposite direction with such a train ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.114b
sections.original: Húc .
sections.translation: To this place ( pointing to the house .)

sections.label: Section 1.2.114c
sections.original: Quid huc ? quis istíc habet ?
sections.translation: Why to this place ? Who lives there ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: Amor , Voluptas , Venus , Venustas , Gaudium ,
sections.translation: Love , Pleasure , Venus , Beauty , Joy , Jesting , Dalliance , Converse , and Sweet-kissing .

sections.label: Section 1.2.117
sections.original: Quid tibi commercist cum dis damnosissimis ?
sections.translation: What intercourse have you with these most destructive Deities ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.118
sections.original: Mali sunt homines , qui bonis dicunt male ;
sections.translation: Bad are those men who speak evil of the good . You speak not well of even the Gods themselves ; you do what is not right .

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: An deus est ullus Suavisaviatio ?
sections.translation: Is Sweet-kissing , then , some God ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.121
sections.original: An non putasti esse umquam ? o Lyde , es barbarus ;
sections.translation: And do you not think she is ? O Lydus , why , what a barbarians [*] you are , you , whom I had deemed to be far more wise than Thales hinself [*] . Go to , you are more foolish than Potitius , the foreigner [*] , who , at an age so advanced , knew not the names of the Divinities .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: Non hic placet mi ornatus .
sections.translation: This dress of yours pleases me not .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125b
sections.original: Nemo ergo tibi
sections.translation: But no one prepared it for you ; it was prepared for myself , whom it pleases well .

sections.label: Section 1.2.127
sections.original: Etiam me advorsus exordire argutias ?
sections.translation: And do you commence upon your repartees against myself even ? You , who , if you had even ten tongues , ought to be silent .

sections.label: Section 1.2.129
sections.original: Non omnis aetas , Lyde , ludo convenit .
sections.translation: Not every age , Lydus , is suited for school [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: magis unum in mentemst mihi nunc , satis ut commode
sections.translation: One thing especially is just now on my mind , how the cook may with due care attend to these things as befits the elegance of the entertainment .

sections.label: Section 1.2.132
sections.original: Iam perdidisti te atque me atque operam meam ,
sections.translation: Now have you undone yourself and me and all my labours , me who so oft have shown you what is right , all to no purpose .

sections.label: Section 1.2.134
sections.original: Ibidem égo meam operam perdidi , ubi tu tuam :
sections.translation: In the same place have I lost my labour where you ’ ve lost yours : your instructions profit neither me nor yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.2.136
sections.original: O praeligatum pectus .
sections.translation: O obdurate heart !

sections.label: Section 1.2.136b
sections.original: Odiosus mihi es .
sections.translation: You are troublesome to me . Hold your tongue , Lydus , and follow me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.137b
sections.original: Illuc sis vide ,
sections.translation: Now , see that , please ; he no longer calls [*] me tutor , but mere Lydus .

sections.label: Section 1.2.139
sections.original: Non par videtur neque sit consentaneum ,
sections.translation: It seems not proper , nor can it be fit , that ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: cum haec qui emit intus sit et cúm amica accubet
sections.translation: when a person is in a house , and is reclining at the feast together with his mistress , and is kissing her , and the other guests are reclining too , the tutor should be there too in their presence .

sections.label: Section 1.2.143
sections.original: An hoc ad eas res opsonatumst , obsecro ?
sections.translation: Are these provisions purchased for such a purpose , pray ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.144
sections.original: Sperat quidem animus : quo evenat dis in manust .
sections.translation: My intentions , indeed , expect so ; how it falls out , is in the hands of the Gods .

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: Tu amicam habebis ?
sections.translation: Will you be having a mistress ?
